data.title: Organogenesis: Build On Weakness For Big ReNu Knee OA Payday
data.description:
This is my sixth Organogenesis (NASDAQ:ORGO) article, following most recently 10/2024's "Organogenesis Holdings: Solid Finances Protect From Uncertain Environment". At the time I wrote Uncertain Environment, it traded at $4.95; I rated it a "Hold".

It has since dropped ~45%. It had a nice pop following its Q4, 2024 earnings as discussed in Uncertain Environment. It gave back the bulk of these gains when its Q1, 2025 earnings disappointed, as shown by its price chart below:

In this article, I report on Organogenesis' forward prospects. In doing so, I refer to its Q1, 2025 earnings reported on 05/08/2025 per its Q1, 2025 earnings conference Call, 10-Q and its latest website Presentation dated 03/2025.

Organogenesis' dominant wound care franchise underperformed during Q1, 2025.

The table below from its 10-Q shows how Organogenesis' Advanced Wound Care business predominates over its Surgical & Sports Medicine franchise:

For Q1, 2025 compared to Q1, 2024 Wound Care suffered a precipitous falloff in revenues; its Sports Medicine business showed modest growth during this same period.

Both components fared significantly worse than their sterling Q4/Q4 comparisons as shown on its Q4, 2024 press release excerpted below:

It's easy to see why the market reacted so negatively to its Q1, 2025 earnings compared to Q4, 2024. The question investors must resolve in deciding their course of action is, "Can Organogenesis turn this around and return to growth?"

The answer is likely that it is hemmed in for the balance of this year and possibly longer by Medicare's ongoing review of LCD's — Local Coverage Determinations for skin substitute products. The 10-Q explains:

...on January 24, 2025, the MACs [Medicare Administrative Contractor] announced a delay in the implementation of the LCDs until April 13, 2025, and on April 10, 2025, the MACs announced another delay in the implementation of the LCDs until January 1, 2026. Under the new LCDs finalized in November 2024, should they take effect as scheduled, a total of eighteen products would remain covered, including our Apligraf and Dermagraft products for DFUs and VLUs, and our Affinity and NuShield products for DFUs; however, more than 200 products would be classified as “non-covered,” including our PuraPly, PuraPly AM, PuraPly XT, Novachor, TransCyte, Dual and Matrix products for DFUs and VLUs. It is uncertain if there will be further delays in implementing the new LCDs and/or if the new LCDs will be revised or rescinded going forward. If implemented, the LCDs could materially impact utilization of these products, our business, and our revenue. Any future changes or other developments related to these or other LCDs also could affect utilization of our products, our business, and our revenue.

I concluded Uncertain Environment with the following observation:

We live in uncertain times. For Organogenesis, the uncertainties will be compounded by Medicare LCD issues and CMS staffing issues. Organogenesis' strong liquidity and positive cash flow provide its shareholders with critical protection. Accordingly, I rate Organogenesis as a "Hold".

Judging by the 10-Q material referenced above. My uncertainty conclusion was on point, if not an understatement.

Sports Medicine's upcoming ReNu BLA presents an intriguing opportunity.

Osteoarthritis [OA] of the knee presents a huge market opportunity, estimated to exceed $30 billion. Organogenesis has been pursuing this market since its 2017 acquisition of NuTech Medical, which added a variety of products to its pipeline, including ReNu. Organogenesis' 2020 10-K includes the following graphic excerpted from a table of its Surgical & Sports Medicine products describing its ReNu for knee OA:

The year listed 2015 is the year of ReNu's initial launch under the section 361 HCT/P pathway. The dagger and asterisk refer to the notes below:

†

Launched by NuTech Medical; acquired by Organogenesis in 2017.
*

Initially commercialized as a 361 HCT/P but may require BLA approval pursuant to recent 361 HCT/P Guidance from the FDA.

As reported in Organogenesis' 2021 10-K:

On May 31, 2021, we suspended commercial distribution of ReNu in connection with the end of the FDA’s enforcement grace period for certain products that previously were marketed as 361 HCT/Ps. We are continuing to conduct clinical studies of ReNu to support BLA approval for the management of symptoms associated with knee osteoarthritis and, based on feasibility studies that are subject to further evaluation, we believe ReNu may have potential as a treatment for additional osteoarthritis and tissue regeneration applications, which would need to be clinically evaluated further before any such approved uses.

As stated in its most recent 10-K Organogenesis has two recent phase 3 prospective, multicenter, double-blind, randomized, saline-controlled clinical trials to evaluate the efficacy of amniotic suspension allograft (ASA, ReNu) in treatment of OA in the knee.

Presentation slide 12 describes these studies below:

The latest 10-K updates the status of its second phase 3 trial as follows:

... We expect to have all patients completing the study by the end of the second quarter of 2025, and to complete the initial statistical analysis and have top-line data results from the second phase 3 study to share publicly in September 2025.

It goes on to note that it expects to complete its analysis in time to submit its BLA by year-end 2025.

Q1 results have diminished Organogenesis' liquidity.

During the Call, CFO Francisco reported:

As of March 31, 2025, the company had $110.5 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash with no outstanding debt obligations. And that compared to $136 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash with no outstanding debt obligations as of December 31, 2024.

This reflects a significant loss of nearly 20% of its cash. It seems less significant when compared to the ~18% reduction in liquidity from $104.3 million in Q4, 2023 to $89.3 million in Q1 2024 with comparable reductions the previous year.

Its annual cash burn as reflected in net cash provided in operating activities shown in the 10-K was positive for years 2022-2024 as shown by its cash flow table below:

The 10-Q reports Q1, 2025 cash burn of ~$20 million and Q1 2024 cash burn of ~$10 million. In both cases, Organogenesis underperformed during Q1 with a recovery in performance during the remainder of the year.

Organogenesis reports no cash runway information as such. During the Call, Francisco advised:

We expect to see improving cash performance over the balance of 2025 and believe we have the requisite capital to execute our growth strategies with $110.5 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash and $125 million available for future revolving borrowings under our revolving facility.

Based on past performance, there is every reason to expect that cash performance will indeed improve over the balance of the year. Accordingly, when you consider its $110.5 balance plus its $125 million revolving credit, it has ample liquidity to operate its business without any cash raise for at least the next 18 months.

Conclusion

There is a definite possibility, if not likelihood, that Organogenesis improves on its Q1, performance over the balance of the year. However, I consider it as essentially rangebound as reflected by its three-year price chart below:

This chart is unencouraging, particularly when one considers its near term Medicare issues. I am looking for it to break out on the positive side if it reports promising data from its second ReNu clinical trial, discussed above.

If it is able to establish ReNu as a credible FDA approved OA therapy, Organogenesis stands to develop its Surgical and Sports Medicine revenue to equal or exceed its Advanced Wound Care revenue. As noted previously, the market is huge. It is also highly fragmented. A branded therapeutic with substantial clinical support has limitless potential.

The situation is highly uncertain as matters now stand, which is why I rate it as a "Hold". Its situation merits accumulation on weakness for those who are optimistic about ReNu.

data.title: SLVO: Watching Silver Seasonality For Potential Breakout
data.description:
It has been a while since I last wrote about the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (NASDAQ:SLVO). Previously, I was concerned about the design of the SLVO ETN, which tracks the investment performance of owning the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) while writing out-of-the-money ("OTM") call options on the SLV ETF (Figure 1).

In my opinion, investors in the SLVO ETN were forgoing potential capital upside in exchange for upfront premium income. In general, funds and products that use the covered-call strategy employed by SLVO tend to underperform their underlying assets over time, especially during periods of rapid price appreciation. However, measured from my July 2023 article, the SLVO ETN has actually outperformed SLV by 0.3% (Figure 2).

What was the driver of SLVO's outperformance, and is it likely to continue?

In my opinion, SLVO has been able to keep pace with the SLV ETF in the past year because a worsening economic situation has kept a lid on silver prices, despite positive fundamentals driving precious metals higher.

However, with President Trump walking back the most Draconian of his tariffs against China, the most severe economic outcomes have been taken off the table. Furthermore, with Congress working to pass Trump's 'Big Beautiful Bill' that will likely add trillions to the U.S. government's debts, I believe silver prices may be primed for a breakout in the coming months.

While the SLVO ETN may still underperform the SLV ETF in a rapidly appreciating silver price environment, SLVO investors should still do very well on an absolute level. I am upgrading SLVO to a buy recommendation.

Brief Fund Overview

As mentioned above, the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN is an exchange-traded note ("ETN") offered by UBS that gives investors exposure to the Credit Suisse NASDAQ Silver FLOWS™ 106 Index ("Index"), an index that tracks the investment performance of owning the iShares Silver Trust ETF ("SLV") while writing 6% OTM call options on the SLV ETF. Option premiums received from the strategy are paid out to investors as cash coupons every month. Most recently, the SLVO ETN paid a $2.1553/unit distribution in May, which annualizes to a 32.5% distribution yield. In the last twelve months, SLVO has paid a 32.6% distribution yield (Figure 3).

The SLVO ETN has $191 million in assets under management ("AUM") and charges investors a 0.65% expense ratio.

Slow And Steady Wins The Race In the Short Term

In hindsight, the key to SLVO's modest outperformance has been the relative steadiness of silver commodity prices in the past year. In the last twelve months, the SLV ETF, a bullion fund that tracks silver prices, has gained a modest 12.1% with multiple tests of the $30-32 breakout level (Figure 4).

Without large, rapid moves higher in silver prices, SLVO's strategy of selling slightly OTM call options worked out well and allowed it to outperform SLV by 8.4% in the past year (Figure 5)

However, in my opinion, I continue to believe SLVO's strategy will underperform in the long run, since the SLVO ETN cannot keep pace with SLV during periods of rapid price increases. For example, the SLVO ETN has underperformed the SLV ETF by a cumulative 17.3% over the past 5 years, with significant underperformance in 2020 when silver prices appreciated rapidly (Figure 6).

Gold Has Broken Out, Is Silver Next?

Last year, I wrote a bullish article on gold investments, highlighting the multi-year breakout in the price of gold, coinciding with the beginning of a rate-cutting cycle from the Federal Reserve (Figure 7).

Since the breakout from the ~$2000 level, gold prices have soared nearly 70%, as geopolitical tensions (shooting wars and trade wars) and a worsening fiscal situation in the United States have caused investors to bid up the precious metal.

However, one surprising aspect of the current gold rally is the lack of participation in silver prices. Historically, gold and silver prices have a close to 80% correlation (Figure 8).

However, in the past year, the fortunes of these two precious metals have diverged, as gold prices, as represented by the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) have soared higher by 43.5% compared to the more modest appreciation in the SLVO ETN and SLV ETF (Figure 9).

Silver Is More Economically Sensitive

While both gold and silver have historically been used as a store of value, silver also has more industrial uses, such as jewelry, electronics, and solar panels (Figure 10). This makes silver more economically sensitive and prone to underperform gold when economic growth is weak.

As Trump's trade policies threatened the global economy earlier in the year, silver was negatively impacted and lagged the price of gold. However, since Trump has backed off from his most Draconian policies by reducing the tariff rate on Chinese imports, the threat of a steep global recession has also receded. Looking forward, I believe the setup is favorable for silver to play 'catch-up' versus gold in the coming months.

Silver Seasonality Coming Into Focus

In particular, I would like to highlight silver's pronounced tendency to find a tradable bottom during late-June/early-July, for a strong seasonal rally into the Fall (Figure 11).

Investors Losing Confidence In The United States?

While it is difficult to identify the precise driver for silver's pronounced seasonality, this time, the catalyst could be Trump's Big Beautiful Bill ("BBB"), which is working its way through Congress with Trump and Mike Johnson hoping to pass it before the Fourth of July.

The BBB plan extends Trump's 2017 tax cuts while boosting spending for border, deportation, and national defense priorities at the expense of cuts to social safety nets like Medicaid and food stamps. Elon Musk has called the BBB a 'disgusting abomination' filled with Congressional 'pork' that will boost the budget deficit to $2.5 trillion.

Meanwhile, the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office's ("CBO") preliminary analysis suggests the BBB plan will add $3.8 trillion to the federal deficit over the next decade while cutting $698 billion in Medicaid and $267 billion in food stamp benefits.

With America's public debt already on an unsustainable path even before the BBB plan, it is no surprise that Moody's recently downgraded the U.S. government's credit rating (Figure 12).

Increasingly, there is talk in financial markets that global investors are 'losing confidence' in the United States and selling their US-dollar-denominated assets, causing long-term treasury yields to surge to multi-year highs and the U.S. dollar to plunge to multi-year lows (Figures 13 and 14).

If the BBB plan is passed into law by Republicans in Congress, I fear an acceleration in the capital outflow away from US-dollar assets, which should benefit silver prices and the SLVO ETN / SLV ETF.

Risks

While I have a bullish outlook on silver prices and the SLV ETF, I suspect the coming moves in silver markets may be large and swift. Recall from above, the weakness of SLVO's strategy is that it tends to lag when silver appreciates rapidly. If my thesis is correct, I fear the SLVO ETN may be entering a period of significant underperformance against the SLV ETF.

Also, if President Trump reverses his recent tariff pauses and reimpose Draconian tariffs on global trading partners, I believe financial markets may resume their April declines, and silver will underperform gold due to its economic sensitivity.

Conclusion

The SLVO gives investors a handsome distribution along with participation in the appreciation of silver prices.

In my opinion, silver may play 'catch-up' to gold in the coming months, as Trump's 'TACO' approach to tariffs has removed the most severe economic downsides to his trade policies. At the same time, Trump's centerpiece BBB plan may add trillions to the federal deficits, which could accelerate the outflow of capital from US-dollar assets into the relative safety of gold and silver.

In this environment, it is hard to keep a 'sell' rating on the SLVO ETN. While SLVO may still underperform the SLV in a rapidly rising silver price environment, SLVO investors should still do very well. I am upgrading SLVO to a buy rating.

data.title: Shoe Carnival Is Even At Higher Risk After Tariffs, But The Stock Has Not Noticed
data.description:
Shoe Carnival's (NASDAQ:SCVL) Q1-2025 earnings were pretty bad, with comparable sales down at the aggregate level and worsening trends at the flagship Shoe Carnival banner.

Management continues to blame macro pressures for weakness at the core chain, while pointing to Shoe Station’s outperformance as proof of strategy. This contradiction is evident.

Despite rising uncertainty from tariffs and continued weakness in low-income consumer segments, SCVL maintained its full-year guidance.

The stock now trades at 11–12x FY25 earnings, a valuation that looks neither conservative nor attractive. I maintain my Hold rating.

Q1-2025 results

Shoe Carnival struggling: Comps at the Shoe Carnival banner were down 10% YoY, a deterioration from the mid-single digit decline seen last quarter. All categories were down at Shoe Carnival.

Management attributes the performance to macro headwinds, including a soft tax refund season and weak low-income consumer sentiment. But that argument doesn’t hold up when compared to the performance of the company’s other banners.

Shoe Station counter example: Shoe Station posted a 5% comp increase in the quarter. Management noted strength across all categories and emphasized that Shoe Station outperformed in affluent, rural, and lower-income markets.

This makes the macro explanation for Shoe Carnival’s poor results inconsistent. If the same customers are shopping at Shoe Station, and in many cases in the exact same locations, then the underperformance at Carnival points to something deeper: format fatigue, merchandising issues, or brand erosion.

Margins down: With comps down, margin deleverage was inevitable. Merchandise margins were up 50bps, but occupancy deleveraged by 160bps and SG&A by another 210bps, for a total of 400bps in margin erosion. For a retailer already under pressure, this is significant.

Rebanner focus: Instead of preserving cash or leaning into buybacks while the stock trades near 5-year lows, management is doubling down on the Shoe Station conversion plan. In FY25, SCVL will spend $30–40 million in CapEx and take a $20–25 million P&L hit related to store closures, conversion costs, and advertising to support the transformation of 75 Shoe Carnival stores into Shoe Station locations.

Management claims the initiative has a 2–3 year payback, and is accelerating the plan to have over 50% of stores under the Shoe Station banner by mid-2026, and over 80% by early 2027 (according to the call).

Guidance is not conservative: Despite the weak Q1 and a worsening macro backdrop from the sharp increase in tariffs in China and other countries, the company did not revise its full-year guidance.

This feels unrealistic. Guidance still calls for FY25 revenues down 4% to up 2% YoY, and EPS between $1.60 and $2.10. Yet Q1 comps were down 8% overall, and 10% at the core Shoe Carnival banner.

Valuation is not attractive

Since my last article, shares are down about 10%. But that hasn’t changed the valuation profile. At ~$19–20 per share, and with FY25 EPS guidance unchanged at $1.60–2.10, SCVL trades at 11–12x earnings.

That is a high multiple for a U.S. brick-and-mortar retailer facing traffic pressures, margin deleverage, and negative comps, even more so when those comps are deteriorating and the guidance assumes a sharp improvement in the back half of the year against a bad macro outlook.

I would barely consider 11–12x an attractive multiple for a stable retailer in the current US context, let alone one whose core business is shrinking and whose guidance seems aggressive.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold on SCVL.

data.title: GE HealthCare: The Demand Is Just Too Strong To Ignore
data.description:
GE HealthCare (NASDAQ:GEHC) is a relatively recent company, a product of GE's spinoff in 2023. Since then, the stock had tried to give good capital appreciation to its shareholders, but with recent US tariffs instability, the shares plummeted from $94 to $60, and just more recently rebounded to $71. Now, after some dust has settled down, and nice long-term trends like an aging population, I think this price point provides a good opportunity for shareholders to get into the stock.

GE HealthCare's business is quite solid

GE HealthCare's business model is interesting, strong, and diverse. The company has four reportable segments, being a solid leader in three of them, in my opinion. GEHC not only sells products, but also services, which helps the company to be even more robust overall. Let's analyze each division according to its most recent 10K.

The first segment in GEHC is Imaging. Here, the company offers high technology products like Molecular Imaging, Computed Tomography, Magnetic Resonance, Women's Health, and X-Ray products. From these segments, Molecular Imaging and Magnetic Resonance are not commoditized products since their manufacturing and maintenance require high expertise and only a few companies can satisfy the demand globally. These products also benefit from the AI technology boom, due to the important data that these scans provide, making more precise diagnoses, and being even more desirable. The main competition for this segment comes from Philips and Siemens, making this division, in my opinion, a very concentrated oligopoly.

According to its most recent presentation, the Imaging division grew 5% year over year and represents around 40% of total revenues. From a profitability perspective, the segment has an EBIT margin of just 9.3%, which has expanded 130bp from a year ago.

The second segment reported by GEHC is Advanced Visualization Solutions. Here, the firm offers products that are a little bit less advanced technologically than in Imaging, but are still quite important for the markets. This AVS segment is focused on selling ultrasound products and procedural guidance. Competition comes again, mainly from the same two companies that those in the segment before, Philips and Siemens.

The AVS division contributes to 25% of the total company's revenues, and had a 21% EBIT margin in the last quarter, making it a very profitable segment in the company.

The third segment in GEHC is, in my opinion, the most commoditized one: Patient Care solutions. In this segment, the company has a vast catalog of products related to monitoring solutions like telemetry devices, multi-parameter monitors, central stations, and similar. Because of the relatively low-tech nature of this product, the competition here is stronger and has more participants. Main competitors are Medtronic, Philips, Masimo, Stryker, and Philips.

From a revenue perspective, this segment only contributes under 20% of total revenues, and because of its more commoditized nature, the EBIT margins are more volatile and inferior, being now just 6%, from 10% a year ago, for the last quarter.

The last segment in GE HealthCare is Pharmaceutical Diagnostics results. Here, GEHC sells contrast imaging agents, which are substances introduced to the body that help the imaging processes to get clearer results. This segment goes hand in hand with the first Imaging division. Competition in the segment is fierce, with participants like Guerbet, Bracco, and mainly Bayer.

This segment's revenues grew by 8% from a year ago, and correspond to around 15% of the total. Despite this, the EBIT margin is high, over 32%, primarily because these substances are in high demand, and getting authorization for the legal commercialization is difficult and costly.

Overall, the company experiences high demand across its segments. I expect this to continue long-term, since an aging population means that more patients will need to be diagnosed and monitored incrementally over time. For example, the company's book-to-bill ratio right now is at 1.09. This means that there is substantially more demand than GEHC can fulfil the market. Such a high book-to-bill ratio caused the company to have a 10% order increase compared to the same period last year.

Tariffs and the current GEHC status

GE HealthCare is a manufacturing company that depends on supply chain products around the globe. This has helped the company to be more efficient and keep up with competitiveness throughout the years. Unfortunately, the recent tensions caused by the current US administration in terms of tariffs have caused volatility in the company's margins.

For instance, the company had to reduce much of its guidance significantly. While organic revenue growth was kept around 3%, the EBIT margin guidance is expected to be reduced by 210bp to 14.4% from 16.7%. This also impacted adjusted EPS from $4.7 expected to the now revised $4 range, an important fall, that might reset the stock price if the tariffs continue to be high or remain quite uncertain for a while. Of course, the management is trying to combat this policy uncertainty, but it's not really that simple, because of this dependence on global supply chains.

Capital allocation

GEHC has recently acquired the remaining 50% of Nihon Medi-Physics from Sumitomo Chemical. This will bring some $200 million in revenues annually, which is almost immaterial.

Now, talking about the balance sheet, GEHC has some legacy high debt from the original GE. In 2023, the company had an important $8.4 billion debt, which has been reduced to the current $6.7 billion, while leverage ratios have fallen from 2.5 to a more manageable 1.7. This debt is still weighing on the company's earnings, as the company still has to pay more than $400 million annually just in net interest expenses.

That leverage reduction has now allowed GEHC to ramp up its buybacks from just covering the $110 million share dilution, to now really return cash to the shareholders with a starting number for this year of $1 billion, which corresponds to about half of earnings.

Valuation and Possible Returns

GEHC has captured my attention because of the solidity of its business model, but I think that its potential growth despite the tariff challenges is underrated. Now at 17 times forward PE, I think the stock is cheap, considering its strong product demand.

In the following model, I assume GEHC would grow earnings by about 10%, which, I think, is optimistic, but might go in line with the current 4% revenue growth expected. Considering the current 1.09 book-to-bill ratio, the company might still grow revenues faster.

I am also considering $1 billion buybacks for the next years annually, which might give a stronger boost to the EPS growth. For those reasons, I also think a more representative PE ratio of the company's growth might be 20, which, I think, is not overly rich.

Following that, the model brings an impressive 18% CAGR to 2029, signalling strong undervaluation right now, but also compared to its future growth. I am not counting on dividends at this point, and I would prefer that the company does not issue them for better capital appreciation of the stock.

Risks

GEHC is an industrial company that depends on global supply chains to fulfil its orders. Disruptions on those, like the ones caused by pandemics or tariffs, could derail the company's path to satisfy my current investment thesis.

The company is also exposed to macroeconomic black swans, since medical organizations could see some of their budget cut, especially in countries where healthcare is not public.

Interest rates increases and inflationary pressures might also contract profitability margins, generating a significant stock underperformance over time.

Conclusion

GE HealthCare is a pretty solid company with good, decent growth ahead. Even though the company is not running on hot trends like AI or software, the aging population trend is a strong one, and might bring very good results for shareholders from this point.

The stock has some important risks, and potential investors might have to assume further volatility if the current administration continues to generate tariff uncertainty; therefore, even though I think the stock is a buy, potential buyers need to be very well diversified at the moment of buying this kind of globally exposed industrial stock.

data.title: Palantir: Bin The Valuation - This Is A Momentum Stock (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I admit I've been all over the place with Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) for my past few articles. The tariff selloff in the broader markets tanked this stock right after my strong buy rating in February. In March, I gave the stock a strong sell rating, as overvalued stocks (particularly those with a high beta) don't perform well when the S&P 500 is down. My latest rating was a Hold. I wrote the article the day before the 90-day tariff pause, so that rating aged like milk.

This time, the broader markets are on track to return to all-time highs after a turbulent start to the year, which is great news for stocks that have a P/E ratio of 287, like Palantir does.

I have upgraded my rating to a buy, mainly for the following three factors.

First, the chart looks quite attractive after breaking through the $125 support level on its second try in May.

Second, the panic selloff following Q1 2025 results was short-lived. I believe the panic selloff was caused by unrealistic expectations that the company would eviscerate EPS estimates. As seen below, the company was pretty much in line with the analyst consensus, which, I believe, didn't sit well with investors.

Also, the stock was overbought before the Q1 earnings came out, so almost any news gave traders a reason to sell (a classic case of "sell the news"). That said, the company beat its Rule of 40 KPI, scoring 83% in the past quarter. Also, management increased the midpoint of their FY 2025 revenue guidance from $3.75 billion to $3.9 billion. Overall, the results were impressive in pretty much every segment (except the international commercial segment).

Third, the fundamentals and the narrative are still intact despite the panic selloff in the broader markets earlier this year. US enterprises are adopting the AIP platform at an even faster pace than 2024, and the revenue growth in the US government hasn't slowed down despite the Pentagon's $50 billion cost-cutting target by 2026 or DOGE.

Overall, despite absurdly high valuation ratios (EV/EBITDA of 704, P/CF of 233), I rate Palantir as a buy for the reasons noted above. The main risk that readers should keep an eye on is a possible slowdown in the US government segment if cost-cutting initiatives intensify in the second part of this year. Also, the international commercial segment is starting to show signs of weakness, especially in Europe.

US Commercial Growth On Steroids

Even though Palantir's main source of revenue growth comes from the government segment, particularly the US government, which accounted for $373 million out of the $487 million in total government revenue last quarter, I have a strong conviction that the market is still paying more attention to the US commercial segment.

Why?

The growth potential from the adoption of the AIP platform by large enterprises outweighs the limited number of government contracts in the US or abroad. Yes, Palantir signed a huge deal with NATO to provide the Palantir Maven Smart System to 32 member states. However, how many more deals like this one are there to come? At one point, the government segment will reach a plateau. In fact, this is already visible in the chart below, where the US government growth plateaued compared to the US commercial 660 bps sequential improvement.

The annual run rate of the US commercial segment has finally surpassed $1 billion for the first time ever. The number of deals went up significantly, doubling yoy for high ticket contracts above $1 million. In the Q1 earnings call, CRO Ryan Taylor presented a few customer success stories that put into perspective how quickly enterprises are transitioning from boot camps to multi-year 8-figure contracts.

A large healthcare company did a boot camp with us in December, and five weeks later, converted to a five-year, $26 million ACV enterprise agreement. A global bank started a pilot with us in Q4 2024, signed a $2 million engagement a month later, then expanded to a three-year, $19 million dollar ACV engagement four months after that. A Fortune 500 healthcare company began working with us in Q2 2024 and last quarter signed a five-year $10 million ACV conversion deal.

To put things into better perspective, I included below a chart, showing the quarterly revenue growth (and revenue value) in the US commercial segment for the past three years. Keep in mind that, according to the CRO, the first quarter is usually the "slowest quarter due to business seasonality". Still, Q1 revenue this year obliterated Q4 numbers.

You may be wondering what is going on the other side of the Atlantic. Why there isn't a European commercial segment? A hint can be found in CEO Alex Karp's statement during the last earnings call.

Europe, which is going through very structural change and doesn't quite get AI, maybe in the near future will get AI, we are hoping and praying,

Well (long sigh), Alex is completely right. Europe is anemic with AI adoption and, as a European working in a multibillion-dollar corporation, I can confirm his statement.

Insider And Institutional Activity

As usual, insiders are either exercising or selling their shares. Not a surprise considering the increase in stock-based compensation in Q4 2024.

That said, finally an insider decided to purchase shares in the open market. It was Planishek Heather, buying $1.16 million worth of shares in early May. Even though I highly favor this type of activity, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne considering her selling activity in the last 12 months.

Aside from the disappointing insider activity, funds have been loading the truck in Q1 this year. The data in Whale Wisdom shows an increase in the number of funds holding this stock. The number of shares owned by 13F filers increased sequentially, despite the selloff we saw in the first quarter. Speaking of the selloff, I believe that is the reason behind the 80% sequential increase in closed positions.

Risks

The anemic growth in Europe was the main driver behind the decline in international commercial revenue in Q1. Sequentially, revenue in this segment declined in the double digits, which is not a particularly positive sign.

I don't think there is any remedy outside of the US, as the mentality of middle and upper level management in large enterprises is completely different on this side of the Atlantic.

Other risks to consider are tied to the US government segment. Even though the revenue of this segment in the past quarter ($373M) outshadowed the non-US government revenue ($114M), there is a stagnation in the growth rate. As shown in one of the charts in the previous section, Q4 2024 growth in this segment was 44.7% yoy vs 45.3% in Q1 this year. While still growing at a spectacular pace, the growth rate has stabilized. This was one of the topics discussed during the Q&A. Here is Shyam Sankar's reply:

And so, we welcome DOGE. I mean, to me, these guys are heroes. They are world-class technologists that could be choosing to do anything, and instead, they are choosing to do this.

Look, there is no doubt that the Gotham and the Foundry platforms are key in supporting agencies making sense of large volumes of structured and unstructured data. However, I can't help but question whether these tools are truly essential for daily operations. If they're just "nice to have," they could be the first to go when budgets get tight.

I recommend keeping a close eye on the growth in the US government segment in the upcoming quarters. Any signs of a slowdown could be an indication that DOGE and DoD cost-cutting initiatives are negatively affecting Palantir's ability to secure new contracts or renew existing ones.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I believe the stock is a buy despite sky-high valuation ratios. To be clear, I don't think the market is looking at the valuation ratios in the table below to decide whether to buy the stock or not.

In my view, the narrative plays a more fundamental role, particularly when the broader markets (i.e. the S&P 500) are in a recovery phase, back to all-time highs prior to the tariff drama earlier this year. Given that the growth story is spotless (except for the international commercial segment), I believe the stock is a buy. That said, I'd keep an eye on the US government segment for any signs of slowdown due to DOGE or DoD cost-cutting initiatives.

data.title: BILL Holdings: Upgrade To Hold Due To Positive Developments
data.description:
Summary

I gave a sell rating to BILL Holdings (NYSE:BILL) in April because demand was softening, and the macro headwinds were likely to put more pressure on growth. Most of my concerns have stayed true. That said, there are positive developments that, I think, are enough to shift to a more balanced view. As such, I am upgrading to a hold rating.

Lingering Macro Headwinds and SMB Weakness

My earlier concerns about SMB weakness are still playing out. In both the 3Q25 earnings call and recent investor conferences in May (JPM TMT and Jefferies), management made it clear that macro uncertainty is still impacting them. It was even explicitly noted by the CFO that there are clear signs of reduced discretionary spending.

"So just at the highest level, we've seen some clear signs that small businesses are reacting to the environment that they're operating in. there's uncertainty" Jefferies conference.

Given that the Jefferies conference was just last week, I think it's fair to say that BILL has not seen any meaningful sequential improvement vs. 2Q25. My view is still the same, that the unclear fiscal policy, trade tensions, and rates uncertainty are translating into softer transaction activity. This can be clearly seen in BILL's 3Q25 numbers, the soft 4Q25 guidance, and the downgrade to FY25 revenue guidance. If that is not enough, it was also explicitly stated by management that total payment volume [TPV] per customer is likely to stay constrained in the near term, which basically tells me that spending patterns aren't seeing any form of recovery just yet.

At the broader level, my opinion is that BILL's near-term growth is still very much driven by how the macro landscape develops. Until SMB confidence returns, it's hard to see a clear path to acceleration.

Monetization Headwinds and Virtual Card Adoption Friction

On top of the broader SMB weakness, I think the monetization outlook for BILL is also getting murkier and is one that further limits near-term upside. While 3Q25 saw a nice sequential uptick in AP/AR (accounts payable/receivable) take rates, management guided to a flat take rate in 4Q25, which marks a meaningful shift from the previous expectations of continuous take rate expansion. This is not a good sign, and it's not because of the guidance but because of the underlying factors. If the downgrade in guidance was due to management being cautious, that is fine. But the reality is, as per the May Jefferies conference, it was due to fewer transactions per customer in some verticals, discretionary spend pullbacks, and uncertainty around trade policy affecting international payments. In other words, the same macro headwind I noted above is becoming a headwind in BILL's ability to monetize its customers.

What complicates things further is the virtual card adoption issue. The idea behind virtual cards is solid in theory, as it offers more control and security for payers. But on the supplier side, the incentive is less obvious. The issue (per management) comes down to interchange fees. These fees are higher than what suppliers pay for ACH or checks, and that cost hurdle is a big friction I see in getting more suppliers to adopt virtual card payments.

To offset this weakness, BILL is pushing harder on its broader ad-valorem suite (things like cross-border payments with FX components, instant transfers, and invoice financing). The idea is that these solutions can help lift take rates over time as they generate revenue as a percentage of transaction value rather than a flat fee.

However, I wonder if adoption of these solutions will be strong enough to fully offset the drag from slow virtual card adoption. While management noted that these newer offerings are key to driving monetization, they noted that they also said take rate expansion might take longer to play out. What this suggests is that even management is not confident a near-term recovery in take rate is likely, I believe. That said, I wouldn't completely write off the potential of these new solutions being take-rate accretive over time (probably not in the next 6 months, but over the next 3 to 5 years). Some of the numbers are hard to ignore. For instance, it was shared on the 3Q25 call that instant transfer and invoice financing have already hit around $10 billion in cumulative payment volume. The Divvy corporate card is also seeing solid early traction.

Two developments that made me less negative

There are two main reasons for my upgrade to a hold rating. The first key is around pricing and go-to-market [GTM] focus. After two years of no pricing changes, BILL finally announced price increases for its core ACH and check payment services, and these new prices went into effect for new customers in March 2025 and are now rolling out to existing customers starting in May. While the 4Q25 revenue impact will be minimal, the bigger takeaway here is that management is willing to pull the pricing lever, and this could help drive near-term upside if they decide to be more aggressive.

Meanwhile, BILL is stepping on its GTM strategy, particularly within its high-value accountant channel, and there are numbers to back it up- In the 3Q25 earnings call, management shared that net new customer additions from accountants were up >60% y/y. I also believe it is a right move for BILL to build out a dedicated sales team focused on selling to suppliers. This is important because it can help BILL to accelerate adoption of its higher-yield offerings (those mentioned above), which is needed to lift take rates.

The second reason is the progress seen in BILL's ecosystem partnerships strategy. The highlight is the Xero integration. The integration enables BILL's bill pay functionality to be embedded directly into Xero's platform, and the benefit for BILL is that it gains direct access to Xero's US SMB subscriber base. I thought this was a solid move, as it is a cheap, fast, and effective way to directly access a large pool of small SMBs that are BILL's core customer group. If BILL manages to capture demand effectively here, it could be a real boost to growth.

These two sets of developments are very promising developments that make the growth outlook much better, especially if the economy turns for the better. And I think they recommend a Hold rating.

Valuation

As much as I was doubtful that BILL could grow revenue by 13% (per consensus previously) for FY25, the 9M25 data shows that it is well on track to achieve that expected growth. The question is then whether BILL can sustain this low-teens growth going into FY26. Given the positive developments, I now see the potential for this to happen, and the probability goes up if the macro environment gets better (this is a wild card, so call it a fifty-fifty chance). Suppose the demand backdrop gets better, and the positive developments work out as expected, BILL could easily meet the growth expectation for FY26 (~13%), and in this case, the stock should continue to trade at 20x NTM PE (the current valuation). The existence of this upside risk means that recommending a sell rating is not sensible, especially since nobody knows how the macro environment is going to play out. As such, I am upgrading to a hold rating to sidestep this uncertainty.

Conclusion

My upgrade to a hold rating on BILL is because there are positive developments that made me believe BILL can sustain low-teens growth in FY26. I may be wrong, but if BILL does grow as expected, the stocks should continue to trade at the current multiple. As much as I still think SMB demand is still weak, the current risk and reward looks more balanced; hence, I think staying on the sidelines is the right call.

data.title: Day One Biopharmaceuticals: Strong Ojemda Potential In Q2 And Solid Rationale For DAY-301
data.description:
Day One Biopharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:DAWN) is now a year into the launch of Ojemda (tovorafenib) for the treatment of pediatric low-grade glioma, but recent earnings updates failed to stimulate a rally in the stock. I rated the name a buy in April based on the potential of Ojemda now, and in the future, and the recent earnings provides an opportunity to review the launch. In this article, I confirm my buy rating, as the company hints at a possible strong Q2'25 ahead for Ojemda. Further, looking deeper into DAY-301, we can see there is some de-risking thanks to development of a related drug previously.

Ojemda launch: Q1'25 update

With Q1'25 earnings in May, DAWN reported that Ojemda brought in $30.5M in net product revenue for the quarter, which is an 11% increase from Q4'24. For a drug only four quarters into the launch, that isn't a hugely compelling rate of growth. Still, the underlying script growth of 16% is a little better, and I see it as quite unlikely a launch has topped out just a few quarters into the process.

Given the share price didn't rally in response to earnings, that could set up the potential for a bigger move when Q2'25 earnings are reported, in response to confirmation that we might be seeing something more encouraging in terms of growth. Of course, if the company reports more of the same middling growth, or even a lack of growth, just a handful of quarters into the launch, then the stock could continue to trade sideways or even fall. That being said, based on the Chief Commercial Officer's comments on the Q1'25 earnings call, Q2'25 had made a good start.

We delivered well this quarter, despite some seasonality we observed in January. This was primarily due to the holidays impacting the timing of scans and initiating new treatments, which led to a slower start to the year. Since then, we have seen a rebound with April being one of the strongest months for new patient starts since launch.

Lauren Merendino, CCO at DAWN, Q1'25 earnings call. Emphasis mine.

Ojemda worldwide and DAY-301

To go beyond Ojemda in the US, the company has a couple potential catalysts ahead. Firstly, tovorafenib is now under review by the European Medicines Agency thanks to Ipsen (OTCPK:IPSEY) submitting for approval in that geography. However, even if approval and subsequent launch triggers a milestone and the beginning of potential royalties, that event isn't coming in Q2'25, but perhaps in 2026, given IPSEY submitted its Marketing Authorization Application on March 27, 2025.

We are also eligible to receive up to approximately $350.0 million in additional commercial launch and sales-based milestone payments based on exchange rates as of the reporting date. We are also entitled tiered, double-digit royalty payments starting at mid-teens percentage of annual net sales of tovorafenib, subject to customary adjustments specified in the Ipsen License Agreement.

Comments from DAWN, recent 10-Q.

Beyond Ojemda, the company is continuing with dose escalation work of its PTK7-targeting ADC, DAY-301. There isn't much of an update since the company passed the first dose level without issue in January, although since it could take a few months to pass each dose level, and doses often start fairly low, we could be waiting several quarters for something high impact from that work.

Indeed, the company didn't provide guidance for a readout from the work, which seems reasonable, given the company doesn't know for sure when it'll start to see substantial efficacy or dose-limiting toxicity.

In the meantime, in April 2025, a publication in the journal Lung Cancer reported phase 1b data from cofetuzumab pelidotin, another PTK7-targeting ADC in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The 2025 publication provides a more granular analysis of an earlier 2023 report, with a few more patients and a longer follow up. The 2023 report noted from 56 patients with NSCLC treated with cofetuzumab pelidotin, there was a 19.6% objective response rate (ORR), although this number jumped to 30% in the 20 evaluable patients with non-squamous, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) wild-type, high PTK7 expression group. Unfortunately, in the 2025 report, the response rate in that group (non-squamous, EGFR WT, high-PTK7 expression) dropped 21.4% from 28 evaluable patients.

Perhaps more interesting in the first-in-human data from cofetuzumab pelidotin from a 2021 report in multiple tumor types including ovarian cancer, NSCLC and triple-negative breast cancer. That report did demonstrate that even complete responses can be seen in ovarian cancer patients that have had prior treatment.

Further, the 2021 report notes no grade 5 adverse events. Throughout the phase 1 work, neutropenia was seen, however, for example, grade 3 or higher neutropenia occurred in 39% of patients in the phase 1b data reported in 2025. Indeed, the 2025 publication noted that neutropenia is commonly seen with auristatin payloads, and cofetuzumab pelidotin is an auristatin-based ADC. Auristatins tend to be microtubule inhibitors and Aur0101, the payload of cofetuzumab pelidotin is indeed a microtubule inhibitor. PTK7 (formerly MTX-13) instead has an exatecan payload, which functions as a topoisomerase I inhibitor, so a different side effect profile would be expected, and differing efficacy could be seen too (not to mention other differences between PTK7 and cofetuzumab pelidotin).

I don't know if there is a one-size-fits-all solution to payloads, but the idea that DAWN has an ADC with validation of the target, thanks to the work with cofetuzumab pelidotin, but potentially a better payload, is an obvious possible conclusion.

Financial Overview

DAWN reported $30.5M of net product revenue in Q1'25 and $0.3M of license revenue. For Q1'25; Cost of product revenue was $2.9M, R&D expense was $39.6M and SG&A expense was $29.3M. DAWN reported a net loss of $36M for Q1'25, with net cash used in operating activities of $59M for the first three months of 2025. The company finished the quarter with $473M in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments. DAWN would be out of cash in eight quarters from the end of Q1'25 at the current rate of cash burn, but revenues from Ojemda continuing to increase can change that. Indeed, the company doesn't expect to have to raise cash again.

We remain well-positioned financially with a cash balance of $473 million and no debt at the end of the first quarter. As we continue to grow, our ability to balance disciplined financial management with measured investment is what sets us apart. Based on our current operating plans, we do not project Day One will require any additional financing in the future.

Charles York, COO & CFO at DAWN, Q1'25 earnings call.

There were 101,362,468 shares of DAWN's common stock outstanding as of May 1, 2025. DAWN has a market cap of $710.6M ($7.01 per share).

Risks

There are several risks to any long in DAWN, some of which are discussed here. Firstly, if DAWN announces delays in European approval, or outright rejection of their drug, the stock could fall, particularly since approval is probably expected given US approval, and the data in FIREFLY-1.

Secondly, if DAWN announces middling growth in Ojemda sales from Q1'25, despite noting a strong April on the Q1'25 conference call, the stock could sell off. Indeed, Ojemda might not grow at all or sales might decline, despite a strong April.

Lastly, if DAWN announces dose-limiting toxicities or a lack of efficacy from its trial of DAY-301, and discontinues development, the value of that asset would be viewed as zero, and the stock would likely tumble.

Conclusion and rating

Seeking Alpha's valuation tab notes DAWN is currently trading with an EV/Sales (TTM) of 1.48, compared to a sector median of 3.21. Not bad for a drug only completing its first year of sales. A major part of the bull case is simply that the company is trading fairly cheap via that metric. There is also the potential for upwards revisions on future estimates of revenues if Q2'25 confirms a resumption of higher growth from quarter-to-quarter, and indeed the company seems to have hinted at that on the Q1'25 conference call. Further, the potential for approval of Ojemda in Europe can provide another source of revenues, via milestones and royalties, and I expect approval given the data seen in FIREFLY-1, and the need for options in this patient population. Lastly, with DAY-301, the company is targeting a validated target, PTK7, but with potentially a better payload. For these reasons, I currently rate DAWN a buy (I'm long the stock).

data.title: Labor Pains Without The Epidural
data.description:
Wall Street spent Wednesday wrapped in a shawl of soft data and political shouting, unsure whether to brace for frost or flirt with summer. The market didn’t rally, didn’t retreat - just stood there, sniffling. Providing the soundtrack: President Trump’s latest ballad, “Too Late Powell,” performed in all caps.

Level Change 6/4/25 (%)

-0.2 Dow

+0.3 Nasdaq

+0.3 Nasdaq 100

+0.0 S&P 500

-0.2 S&P 400

-0.3 S&P 600

May’s ADP report landed with the grace of a brick in a birdbath: just 37,000 private payrolls, a miss of 93,000 and the weakest showing since March 2023. Wages held their ground, but it’s hard to admire a flat paycheck when the job offers stop arriving. With Friday’s government report on deck, traders are bracing for a possible miss, and more tweets.

What once looked like a strategic pause for the labor market now looks more like an unplanned intermission. As indeed economist Allison Shrivastava put it, “the market can’t keep steadily cooling off forever before it just turns cold.” Wednesday’s numbers suggests we might be approaching that temperature threshold.

Trump, never one to miss a Fed critique opportunity, immediately pounced on the ADP release with characteristic subtlety:

“ADP NUMBER OUT!!! ‘Too Late’ Powell must now LOWER THE RATE. He is unbelievable!!! Europe has lowered NINE TIMES!”

The exclamation points did their work - Treasuries rallied as markets contemplated whether Fed Chair Powell’s spine might finally soften under pressure and prose.

The ISM Services index joined ADP in the cooling chorus, slipping back into contraction for the first time in almost a year. New orders nose-dived nearly six points to their weakest since December 2022, while price pressures reached their highest since the following November, an elegant symmetry if you like your pain evenly spaced. The one bright spot: employment crept back into expansion, a single green pixel in an otherwise bleak screen.

Survey comments read like excerpts from Tariff Cujo, a horror anthology in which HVAC systems go feral and timelines vanish in the fog.

One construction firm summed up the mood: “Tariff variability has thrown residential construction supply chains into chaos... Major HVAC manufacturers are passing on cost increases from steel and refrigerant hikes. Planning is difficult for community projects that could be scheduled for the next 22 to 30 months.” Logistics firms called the whole setup a “moving target.”

If this is a soft landing, the seat cushions are on fire.

Meanwhile, the Fed’s Beige Book declared economic activity had “slightly” declined, a word serving as a tranquilizer dart for the anxious. Hiring was stable, but businesses flagged rising concerns about - you guessed it - rising prices.

In Washington, the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office scored the GOP’s reconciliation bill with all the warmth of a hospital invoice: $2.42T added to the deficit over the next decade. Critics called the bill a fiscal fantasy. Supporters called the CBO “lefty.” The bill’s promised savings are still waiting on a calculator that runs on faith.

Still, the S&P kept its chin up, managing a third straight gain and up nearly 20% since April’s Liberation Day low. Investors, it seems, are betting that bad data will beget good policy, or at least easier money. Come on, “too late” Powell. Learn to blink on cue.

Whether that’s wisdom or wishful thinking will become clearer Friday. Until then, markets remain in wait-and-wince mode.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JPIE: Checking-In On One Of Our Holdings
data.description:
Thesis

JPMorgan Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JPIE) is an active fixed income ETF that we hold in our portfolio. The name is a core holding for us, and we last covered the fund in late 2024. In today's article we are going to re-visit the name, its performance, changes in the portfolio composition and strategy as well as our view on the fund given the current macro context.

A good start to the year

JPIE has posted a healthy start to the year, with a 2.85% total return:

We are comparing the fund to several other active ETFs with similar duration, namely the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC), the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) and the iShares MBS ETF (MBB). JPIE has outperformed so far the utilized cohort, albeit by a small margin.

If we look back at the ETF's historic performance, we will notice the name only lost money in its inception year when rates moved up aggressively:

  • 2022: -6.13%
  • 2023: +7.07%
  • 2024: +6.32%
  • 2025: +2.85% (YTD so far)

More importantly, the fund had a very shallow drawdown during the April 2025 market sell-off, with the ETF down only -1% from a total return perspective (chart above). If we look at pure price performance for a 'clean' drawdown profile, we get the following:

On a price basis, the fund's drawdown was slightly higher at -1.6% (from +0.8% to -0.8%), but in-line with its very conservative risk metrics of 2.37% annualized volatility and 2.1% standard deviation, which an investor can find on the 'Risk' tab in the Seeking Alpha platform.

Portfolio managers are keeping the duration profile low

The portfolio duration is now down to 2.83 years as per the fund's analytics:

Duration represents a portfolio's sensitivity to interest rates, and the lower the duration profile, the lower the impact of rising rates on the fund. We like this profile because it is unclear what will happen to rates, especially long rates. Front rates (2- and 3-year) are very well correlated to Fed Funds, so they will eventually move lower. However, intermediate and long rates might go a bit higher if inflation spikes again. We articulated this view extensively in a different research article, namely the 'GOVI: Back To The Future' piece.

The JPIE ETF is active, meaning that the portfolio is changing as per the manager's direction and views, but it is a rates-driven one:

Via its high-quality composition, the fund takes very little credit risk. We can see that below investment grade paper represents only 23% of the holdings, with mostly BB names accounting for that slice. The vast majority of assets are AAA, and we can see from the sectoral parsing that these are MBS bonds:

Agency MBS securities represent 36% of the portfolio, followed by CMBS at 19% and HY Corporates at 14%. The fund looks for low duration assets that provide for a spread to treasuries, and takes little credit risk. We clearly saw this composition kick-in during the April 2025 turmoil, with the drawdown very shallow.

Fund forward

With a 30-day SEC yield of 6.3%, the ETF represents a good vehicle to realize carry (i.e., interest income). The fund's build supports that view, with an overweight positioning in high quality, low duration assets that offer a higher spread than risk-free assets. The name has very shallow drawdowns, and has rates as its main risk factor. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 6% for 2025, in-line with prior figures and the fund composition. An aggressive Fed that would unexpectedly cut several times in 2025 would provide for an additional +3% if 3-year rates move lower by 100 bps.

While some might characterize JPIE as 'boring', the name represents a core component of a balanced portfolio, offering very high returns for the units of risk it takes. Investors should understand they will not make 10% plus per annum here with JPIE, but should expect returns in the 6% to 8% bracket, dependent on rates movements and portfolio manager's trading acumen.

The market has a very favorable view on JPIE, with a steady increase in AUM since inception:

Other analytics for the fund are as follows:

  • AUM: $3.8 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.39%
  • 30-day SEC yield: 6.3%
  • Portfolio yield: 6.46%
  • Duration: 2.8 years

Conclusion

JPIE is an active fixed income exchange-traded fund. The vehicle comes from J.P. Morgan, and has seen its AUM soar since inception. We like the fund's low duration of 2.8 years and its active approach to portfolio construction. Furthermore, we are of the opinion that the front of the yield curve is attractive currently, with spiking deficits impacting intermediate and long rates rather than front yields. JPIE is not an exciting ETF, but a reliable one. The name had a very shallow drawdown during the April 2025 market meltdown, and is to be perceived as a carry vehicle. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 6% for 2025, and still consider the name a 'Buy'.
